alert.message: Error - internet score has dropped below 90%
alert.name: Current Internet Score alert

panels.description: Internet quality score with a linear trendline over the selected dashboard range.

options.content:
## Internet Quality Score
The Internet Quality Score ranges from 0 to 100 and is calculated from weighted network quality measurements.

### Speedtest scoring disabled
This is the default configuration:

- Packet loss: 60%
- Latency: 15%
- Jitter: 20%
- DNS response time: 5%
- Speedtest: 0%

### Speedtest scoring enabled
If `SPEEDTEST_SCORE_ENABLED=True`, reserve 10% for speedtest and reduce the existing components proportionally so their relative balance stays the same:

- Packet loss: 54%
- Latency: 13.5%
- Jitter: 18%
- DNS response time: 4.5%
- Download speed: 7%
- Upload speed: 3%

Speed is compared against the configured expected line speed, currently 500 Mbps down / 250 Mbps up.

## Packet Loss
The percentage of packets (messages) that are lost when being transmitted over your internet connection. Packet loss should be 0% consistently with occasional spikes during peak internet use. Persistent packet loss could indicate:

- Faulty internal hardware (including cabling or modem)
- Faulty ISP hardware
- ISP capacity problems

## Latency to Anchors
The time it takes to get packets to and from various well-known websites. Higher latency means slower performance, even on a high speed connection. If your latency is consistently high, there could be several causes including:

- Faulty internal hardware (including cabling or modem)
- Faulty ISP hardware
- ISP routing issues

## Jitter
Jitter measures how frequently your latency is changing. High jitter in the red zone can have a negative impact on real-time streaming applications, such as gaming and voice calls. Consistently high jitter could be caused due to:
- Faulty internal hardware (including cabling or modem)
- Faulty ISP hardware
- Overuse of current internet capacity (hitting the limit)

## DNS Response Time
DNS is the protocol used to turn website names into IP addresses. Almost every request to the internet starts with a DNS request, so having fast DNS is vital. This graph compares the response time of your currently configured DNS server against several popular public DNS servers. Slow DNS response could be caused by:

- Poor DNS server performance
- High latency

panels.description: Raw speedtest bandwidth with a linear trendline over the selected dashboard range.

options.content:
## Internet Bandwidth
If you do not see any data you need to explicitly enable the test in the .env file configuration.

This dashboard is configured for a 500 Mbps download / 250 Mbps upload line. Speedtest values turn red when they fall below 50% of the expected line speed:

- Download warning below 250 Mbps
- Upload warning below 125 Mbps

Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can travel across your internet connection at a given time. Less bandwidth than expected can be caused by:

- Other clients down-/uploading data
- Faulty internal hardware, including cabling or modem
- Faulty ISP hardware
- ISP capacity problems
